title: Abuse (Part 4): How to Minister to the Abused
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Abuse 4 - How to Minister to the Abused - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: A Matrix of Possible Abuse Scenarios
subpoints: Abuse occurs across all household types: Christian husband/Christian wife, Christian husband/unbelieving wife, unbelieving husband/Christian wife, and unbelieving husband/unbelieving wife
Each combination involves different spheres of help: the church can be involved where professing Christians are present; civil authorities are always available
The critical question is 'Who is the Christian?' — defined here as someone willing to be identified with and accountable to a local church
Church membership is essential: without accountability structures, the church cannot effectively minister in situations of abuse
Descriptions of what typically drives an abusive husband (worldly view of headship, uncontrolled anger, bitterness) and abusive wife (unhappiness, perceived mistreatment, personal history of anger)

outline.title: The Goal: Beyond Stopping Hurt and Harm
subpoints: The secular approach has two goals: stop the hurt (teach the abused their rights, tough love, taking control) and stop the harm (escape, separation, legal action)
The biblical approach agrees with stopping hurt and harm but adds two more goals: heart holiness and winning the abuser to righteousness
The abuser is also made in God's image with an eternal soul — he or she can also be reached by the Spirit of God
The prevailing assumption in most counseling — including integration models — is that an abuser cannot change; this is more influenced by the sobriety movement than Scripture
Goal: help both the abused and the abuser to be God's kind of person, even in the midst of affliction

outline.title: Where Is God? The Abused Person's Question
subpoints: God will defend the cause of the oppressed — Jeremiah 22:16, Psalm 10, Psalm 82, Psalm 56 all testify to this pattern throughout Scripture
The church has wrongly taught that being a Christian means a smooth life; the Bible never promises exemption from trials
The story of Job: his wife said 'curse God and die,' but Job said 'shall we receive good from God and not also receive evil?'
God promises to sustain us through affliction — not to exempt us from it; Proverbs 3:25-26, Philippians 4:11-13, 1 Corinthians 10:13
Where was God when you were being abused? Right there, doing something good for you — as Psalm 119 demonstrates with the psalmist's affliction leading him deeper into the Word

outline.title: What Does God Want of the Abused?
subpoints: God wants the abused to trust him — 1 Peter 2:19-23 gives Christ as the pattern: when reviled he did not revile in return, when suffering he did not threaten, but entrusted himself to the one who judges justly
God wants the abused to rely on his providential protection and the institutions he has ordained

In the case of the abused wife, God wants her to practice 'aggressive submission' — submission pursued with the conviction that God's design is good, while maintaining the protections of the sixth commandment
Joseph's testimony is instructive: 'You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring about the saving of many lives'
Missionaries who go to dangerous fields are praised; women who choose to remain and witness to an abusive husband deserve the same respect rather than condemnation

outline.title: God's Institutions for Protection
subpoints: God has provided two institutions for the protection of the abused: the church and the civil government
A church that practices church discipline is the best friend of an abused member — it provides guidance, objectivity, clear boundaries, both counseling and caring fellowship
The civil government (police, courts) is also ordained by God; Christians should know the laws of their state regarding abuse
The church should maintain a list of safe houses and willing homes for members who need emergency shelter
All crimes should also be reported to civil authorities, since abuse is both a sin (to be addressed by the church) and a crime (to be addressed by the state)

outline.title: Challenges in Ministering to the Abuser
subpoints: The greatest challenge is uncertainty: Is this person's repentance genuine? Abusers have a known ability to manipulate, blame-shift, offer counter-accusations, and modify behavior for a season
Working with an abuser should involve a team, not one person alone — multiple perspectives help check biases and share the emotional burden
The burden of proof rests on the abuser to demonstrate trustworthiness over time; this process is long and gradual
The church should not declare a person an abuser beyond hope — the Spirit of God is not limited by anyone's sin

Reunification decisions should generally be led by the abused person, though with wise counsel; true repentance involves putting off pride and anger and putting on gentleness, patience, and acceptance of consequences

summary:
Continuing the Abuse series, this fourth session examines how to minister to those who have been abused, opening with a matrix of all possible household configurations — Christian/Christian, Christian/unbeliever, unbeliever/Christian, unbeliever/unbeliever — and what each combination implies for how the church and civil authorities can be involved. The critical determinant is who is a Christian, defined here as someone willing to be identified with and accountable to a local church. The session describes patterns common to abusive husbands and abusive wives across these configurations.

The core of the lesson establishes a goal that goes beyond the secular counseling model. The secular approach aims to stop the hurt and stop the harm. The biblical approach affirms these objectives while adding two others: heart holiness in both the abused and the abuser, and winning the abuser over to righteousness. The prevalent assumption in most counseling today — even in integration models — is that an abuser cannot fundamentally change; Pastor Lyro identifies this as more influenced by sobriety movements than by Scripture. The Spirit of God is not limited by anyone's sin.

The session then addresses the question the abused person is often thinking but may not state: where was God when this was happening? The answer is drawn from Psalm 119 (which was written in the context of affliction and persecution), Psalm 10, Psalm 82, Jeremiah, and the story of Joseph. God will defend the cause of the oppressed — not always in this life, but certainly in the life to come. God promises to sustain us through affliction rather than exempt us from it, and the affliction itself can be a sanctifying instrument. The session also addresses what God wants of the abused person: trust in him and his providential protection, reliance on the institutions he has ordained, and in the case of the abused wife, 'aggressive submission' grounded in the beauty of God's created order while never abandoning the self-protection principle of the sixth commandment.
transcript:
## Introduction: Continuing the Abuse Series

We continue in our series on abuse — not on how to abuse, but how to minister to people who have been abused and to minister to people who are abusing as well. I think both need to be addressed. We have defined abuse. We have sought a biblical balance when considering abuse. Now we want to answer the question: how to minister to the abused — and next, we will consider how to minister to the abuser.

## A Matrix of Possible Household Configurations

I want to start by looking at this matrix. It tells us all the possibilities of abuse in the home. On one axis you have different household configurations: a marriage with a professing Christian husband and a professing Christian wife, a Christian husband with an unbelieving wife, a Christian wife with an unbelieving husband, and an unbelieving husband with an unbelieving wife. On the other axis you have the possible spheres of help: the church can be involved where there are professing Christians; civil authorities are always available.

It is important to discern the difference between homes — Christian and non-Christian — and to ask: who is the Christian? This is a critical question because it determines our scriptural approach to the problem of abuse. The definition of Christian I am working with here is the person's willingness to be identified with the church and be accountable through membership at a church. The Scriptures do not have a category for someone who professes to follow Christ while remaining independent from a local church. And when people do that, they separate themselves from the most powerful organization for helping them in these areas. That is why churches without a strong membership policy will be weak and ineffective in dealing with abuse.

**Abusive Christian husband / Christian wife:** What might be leading a husband to abuse? A worldly view of headship — he believes his role is to make his wife submit rather than to serve. He likely has a pattern of overt anger over adverse circumstances: finances, job, sexual problems, health issues. He harbors bitterness toward his wife over some past grievance, and in every argument everything keeps coming up — the mark of unresolved bitterness. And in essence, his wife is not his glory (1 Corinthians 11 — the wife is the glory of the husband). He has forgotten her elevated place in his life.

**Abusive Christian wife / Christian husband:** This exists, though it is less common. She often defines her husband as the chief cause of her unhappiness. She is bitter over a real or perceived mistreatment — a daughter, financial failure, lack of leadership, lack of spiritual depth. She often claims years of neglect of her emotional needs. There is usually a personal history of anger on her part.

**Christian husband / unbelieving wife where the husband is abusive:** This is rare but not impossible — we should never say we are incapable of a particular sin. The key is to address the heart issues driving the aggressive anger: loss of patience, defensiveness of his Christianity toward his unbelieving wife, financial pressures. Key texts to bring to him: 1 Peter 3:7 (*husbands live with your wives in an understanding way*) and Colossians 3:19 (*husbands love your wives and do not be harsh with them*) — no qualification about whether your wife is a believer. If he is unwilling to change, formal church discipline is appropriate as a testimony both to him and to his unbelieving wife.

**Unbelieving husband / Christian wife where the husband is abusive:** Probably the most common scenario. Her very godliness often incites his anger, because the unbelieving world is not neutral but actively against the gospel. This woman needs to know God's protection, the church's protection, and the civil authorities' protection. She needs internal fortification from the Word — she needs to know that God listens to the pleas of the oppressed.

**Unbelieving husband / Christian wife where the wife is abusive:** The unbelieving husband will not put up with a violent Christian wife very long — so we do not often see this because the marriage ends quickly. If she is willing to receive help from the Bible, there is great hope for real change. She needs to carefully examine her heart and live out 1 Peter 3:1-6.

**Unbelieving household:** The most common situation overall. Biblical help is limited — evangelism is going to be necessary first. We do want to make sure everybody is safe and minister to them as best we can, but there is no hope for heart-level change until trust is placed in Jesus Christ.

Any questions on the matrix?

## The Goal: Heart Holiness, Not Just Harm Reduction

So what is our goal in helping the abused and the abuser? Let us assume that the immediate considerations for safety have already been taken care of — the urgent needs, the safe housing, the involvement of civil authorities. Now everyone is safe. What do we do?

The secular approach — and also some integration approaches in the church — typically has two goals: stop the hurt, and stop the harm. Stop the hurt means: teach the abused person their rights, teach self-assertion, teach them to take control of their life. Stop the harm means: escape to shelter, separate from the spouse, encourage legal action. We want those things too.

But our goals go further. We want heart holiness in both the abused and the abuser. And we want to win the abuser over to righteousness. We should not make this either/or — either we minister to the abused or to the abuser. We do both. That guy who abused her is also made in the image of God. He also has an eternal soul. He is also going to go to heaven or to hell. The Spirit of God is not limited by his abuse. Why are we eager for God to change us but not eager for God to change him? We all deserve what's coming to us — and God, in his mercy, doesn't give us what we deserve. That mercy is not just for us.

The prevailing approach in even Christian counseling today is that an abuser is condemned to remain an abuser. That is more influenced by sobriety movements like AA — the idea that you are forever identified by your sin rather than by the Lord Jesus Christ — than it is from Scripture.

So our goal is: help both the abused and the abuser to be God's kind of person, even in the midst of trial. Psalm 119 is a beautiful text here. The whole psalm is written in the context of affliction, and look at what the psalmist says:

*Before I was afflicted I went astray, but now I keep your word.* Affliction can have a sanctifying effect. *You are good and do good; teach me your statutes.* The abused person needs to see that God is still good and still does good. *The insolent smear me with lies, but with my whole heart I keep your precepts.* Even through verbal abuse, the psalmist pursued the precepts of God. *I know, O LORD, that your rules are righteous, and that in faithfulness you have afflicted me.* *It is good for me that I was afflicted, that I might learn your statutes.* What can help the abused person see that God is teaching them through this, keeping them close, not absent?

And Joseph's testimony at the end of Genesis: 'You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring about the saving of many lives.' What is one of our goals as we minister to the abused? To help them be God's kind of person even in the midst of affliction.

Our goal is also to seek to glorify God and win the abuser over to righteousness. And we need to keep in mind the important distinction: a home with Christians present has access to the gospel and to the church in a way an unbelieving home does not. But in both cases our focus is the glory of God for the good of both the abused and the abuser.

## Where Is God? Helping the Abused Find Hope

A big question that comes up — the abused person may not always state it directly, but it is there: where was God in all of this? And where is God now?

It is important that we help them see that God will defend the cause of the oppressed. Jeremiah 22:16: *He judged the cause of the poor and needy — is not this to know me? declares the LORD.* God knows the poor and the needy when he judges their cause. Psalm 10: *LORD, you hear the desire of the afflicted; you will strengthen their heart; you will incline your ear to do justice to the fatherless and the oppressed.* Psalm 82: *Give justice to the weak and the fatherless; maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute; rescue the weak and the needy; deliver them from the hand of the wicked.*

God is the God of the oppressed. He will defend the cause of the oppressed. Now, we must not use this in a Marxist critical-theory sense — always pitting two groups against each other, the oppressed against the oppressor. That is not a biblical way of thinking. But yes, God is there, and God hears.

And we must remember: the Bible does not promise that God will exempt us from trouble. The prosperity gospel and the seeker-friendly movement have given us a version of Christianity in which the Christian life is straight road — no potholes, unicorns and cotton candy. That is not what the Bible says.

Job: his wife said, 'Do you still hold your integrity? Curse God and die.' And Job said, 'You speak as one of the foolish women would speak. Shall we receive good from God, and shall we not receive evil?' And in all this Job did not sin with his lips.

Where is God in the midst of this trouble? God promises that he will supply all that we need during the time of trouble. Proverbs 3:25-26: *Do not be afraid of sudden terror or of the ruin of the wicked when it comes, for the LORD will be your confidence and will keep your foot from being caught.* Philippians 4:11-13: *I have learned, in whatever situation I am, to be content. I know how to be brought low and I know how to abound. In any and every circumstance I have learned the secret of facing plenty and hunger, abundance and need. I can do all things through him who strengthens me.* 1 Corinthians 10:13: *God is faithful, and he will not let you be tempted beyond your ability, but with the temptation he will also provide the way of escape.*

Where is God? He is right there with the abused in the affliction, doing something good even through that.

## What Does God Want of the Abused Person?

He wants us to trust him. 1 Peter 2:19-23: *For this is a gracious thing, when, mindful of God, one endures sorrows while suffering unjustly. For what credit is it if, when you sin and are beaten for it, you endure? But if when you do good and suffer for it you endure, this is a gracious thing in the sight of God. For to this you have been called, because Christ also suffered for you, leaving you an example, so that you might follow in his steps. He committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth. When he was reviled, he did not revile in return; when he suffered, he did not threaten, but continued entrusting himself to him who judges justly.*

That word 'revile' is as close as we can get in English to the original word for verbal abuse. Christ was verbally abused, and he did not revile in return. When he suffered, he did not threaten. He entrusted himself to the one who judges justly. God wants us to rely on his providential protection.

And in the case of an abused wife — and this may be more controversial — God wants her to practice aggressive submission. Now, the sixth commandment implies self-protection; I am not saying a wife should silently endure harm. But it is common in abuse counseling to throw away entirely the notion of submission. To try to solve an unbiblical action by someone using unbiblical ways of thinking is not going to be helpful. We need to be convinced of the beauty of the structures that God has given us and encourage the abused wife to practice submission as much as her circumstances allow, always keeping in mind the sixth commandment. 1 Peter 3:1-6 and 3:13-15 are her calling.

## God's Institutions for Protection

What has God provided for the protection of the abused person? Two institutions: the church and the civil government.

A church that practices church discipline is the best friend of an abused member. There is guidance, objectivity, clear boundaries, both counseling and caring fellowship. People in this congregation have already come to me and said, 'Put me on a safe-house list — I am happy to have someone in my home.' That is the caring fellowship that is needed.

And the civil government — the police and the courts — ordained by God for the protection of the innocent. Christians should know the laws of their state, since jurisdiction will be there. Any crime should be reported to civil authorities. Abuse is both a sin addressed by the church and a crime addressed by the state.

## Ministering to the Abuser: The Challenge of Uncertainty

For the next ten minutes I want to focus on ministering to the abuser.

What do you think is the biggest struggle in helping an abuser? Pride is there. Unwillingness is there. But in my experience and in the literature on abuse, I think the greatest challenge is uncertainty.

Abusers typically have an ability to manipulate. They blame-shift, offer conflicting stories, make counter-accusations, and can modify their behavior for a season. Is the repentance genuine? Is the confession true? You end up, unless there is third-party evidence, with a he-said-she-said situation. The abuser seems sincere. You may even be manipulated. And your own biases — your tendency to read between the lines, to draw premature conclusions, to judge unfairly — all of this is destabilizing.

This uncertainty is disturbing, and if you do not manage it well, it can jeopardize your efforts to help. So it is valuable when working with an abuser to have other people involved with you — not doing it alone. Multiple perspectives help check biases. Multiple people share the emotional and spiritual weight of what is an exhausting ministry. And multiple people help trace the trajectory of the person's change over time so you can stay accountable to one another.

A practical implication: tell the abuser up front that the burden of proof is on them to demonstrate trustworthiness — and that this could take a very long time. Do not say that first. First, when they confess and affirm that what they did was wrong — a sin against God and against the person — affirm that and say: 'I am glad to hear it. Now we start the hard work of showing fruit worthy of repentance.' That makes sense?

Reunification decisions should generally be led by the abused person, though with wise counsel. True repentance involves putting off the underlying sins — pride, anger, selfishness — and putting on the things characteristic of a patient and forgiving person: accepting consequences, accepting the discipline process, giving the abused time to witness the change. No sinner can give 100% assurance that a particular sin will never happen again. That is not how forgiveness works. God does not forgive us only if we guarantee we will never sin again. He forgives upon repentance — and the forgiver incurs the greater cost.

Fathers, thank you for your Lord, you are good, and we thank you that you are in fellowship with your oppressed. We thank you that your Son is gentle and lowly — his yoke is not burdensome. We thank you that we can take his yoke upon us and follow him. We pray that we would meet him even as we worship you this morning. In Jesus' name. Amen.
